does: This paper introduces REBAR, a quantitative evaluation framework that measures the ethical and legal compliance readiness of autonomous systems through rigorous testing. The framework maps operating metrics into an Autonomy Readiness Level (ARL) rubric that numerically quantifies ethical performance across scenarios. Rather than relying on hard-coded behavior restrictions, REBAR employs a neuro-symbolic LLM approach to compute and explain the ethical difficulty of test scenarios, enabling users to assess system limitations and appropriateness for specific tasks. The framework generates computable, interpretable metrics that go beyond existing design-focused approaches, providing transparent measurements of how well autonomous systems navigate ethical constraints.
connects: This work builds on prior research in embodied AI safety and autonomous systems evaluation, extending beyond existing qualitative ethical frameworks that emphasize safety guardrails and red-teaming methodologies. It responds to the limitation that current approaches often provide only binary restrictions without transparency or user override capabilities. The paper connects to parallel research in AI benchmarking and evaluation frameworks, adopting quantitative measurement approaches from ML evaluation while adapting them to the ethical domain. It also draws on neuro-symbolic AI techniques that combine neural networks with interpretable reasoning, reflecting broader trends in developing explainable AI systems. The work inherits challenges from prior standardization efforts in autonomous systems while attempting to formalize previously subjective ethical assessment.
enables: REBAR establishes infrastructure for standardized, comparable evaluation of autonomous system ethics across different platforms and developers, enabling better-informed procurement and deployment decisions. By providing interpretable numerical readiness levels, it opens pathways for regulatory frameworks that can establish minimum ARL thresholds for different applications and risk contexts. The neuro-symbolic approach pioneered here could generalize to other domains requiring formal ethical reasoning, potentially enabling development of certification standards for autonomous systems similar to safety certifications in aviation. Future work could build on this foundation to create hierarchical readiness assessments, integrate real-world incident data into the framework, and develop automated systems that adapt their autonomy based on scenario difficulty ratings, ultimately supporting safer human-AI collaboration in high-stakes domains.
